(November 22, 1924 – June 13, 1987) was an American actress. With a career which spanned four decades across film, stage, and television, Page was the recipient of [numerous accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Geraldine_Page "List of awards and nominations received by Geraldine Page"), including an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award"), a [British Academy Film Award](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Award "British Academy Film Award"), two [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award"), and two [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"), as well as nominations for four [Tony Awards](/wiki/Tony_Awards "Tony Awards").

**Geraldine Sue Page** (November 22, 1924 – June 13, 1987) was an American actress. With a career which spanned four decades across film, stage, and television, Page was the recipient of [numerous accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Geraldine_Page "List of awards and nominations received by Geraldine Page"), including an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award"), a [British Academy Film Award](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Award "British Academy Film Award"), two [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award"), and two [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"), as well as nominations for four [Tony Awards](/wiki/Tony_Awards "Tony Awards").

A native of [Kirksville, Missouri](/wiki/Kirksville,_Missouri "Kirksville, Missouri"), Page studied at the [Goodman School of Drama at the Art Institute of Chicago](/wiki/The_Theatre_School_at_DePaul_University "The Theatre School at DePaul University") and with [Uta Hagen](/wiki/Uta_Hagen "Uta Hagen") and [Lee Strasberg](/wiki/Lee_Strasberg "Lee Strasberg") in New York City. During the [McCarthyism](/wiki/McCarthyism "McCarthyism") era, she was [blacklisted](/wiki/Hollywood_blacklist "Hollywood blacklist") in Hollywood because of her association with Hagen and did not work in film for eight years. Page won the [Academy Award for Best Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actress "Academy Award for Best Actress") for her role in *[The Trip to Bountiful](/wiki/The_Trip_to_Bountiful "The Trip to Bountiful")* (1985). She was Oscar-nominated for her work in *[Hondo](/wiki/Hondo_(film) "Hondo (film)")* (1953), *[Summer and Smoke](/wiki/Summer_and_Smoke_(film) "Summer and Smoke (film)")* (1961), *[Sweet Bird of Youth](/wiki/Sweet_Bird_of_Youth_(1962_film) "Sweet Bird of Youth (1962 film)")* (1962), *[You're a Big Boy Now](/wiki/You%27re_a_Big_Boy_Now "You're a Big Boy Now")* (1966), *[Pete 'n' Tillie](/wiki/Pete_%27n%27_Tillie "Pete 'n' Tillie")* (1972), *[Interiors](/wiki/Interiors "Interiors")* (1978), and *[The Pope of Greenwich Village](/wiki/The_Pope_of_Greenwich_Village "The Pope of Greenwich Village")* (1984). She is also known for her roles in *[What Ever Happened to Aunt Alice?](/wiki/What_Ever_Happened_to_Aunt_Alice%3F "What Ever Happened to Aunt Alice?")* (1969), *[The Beguiled](/wiki/The_Beguiled_(1971_film) "The Beguiled (1971 film)")* (1971) and *[The Rescuers](/wiki/The_Rescuers "The Rescuers")* (1977).

On stage, she made her [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(theatre) "Broadway (theatre)") debut in the 1953 play *Mid-summer*. She went on to receive [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award") nominations for her performances as Princess Kosmonopolis in *[Sweet Bird of Youth](/wiki/Sweet_Bird_of_Youth "Sweet Bird of Youth")* (1959), Marion in *[Absurd Person Singular](/wiki/Absurd_Person_Singular "Absurd Person Singular")* (1974), Mother Miriam Ruth in *[Agnes of God](/wiki/Agnes_of_God "Agnes of God")* (1982), and Madame Arcati in *[Blithe Spirit](/wiki/Blithe_Spirit_(play) "Blithe Spirit (play)")* (1987). For her prolific work onstage she was inducted into the [American Theater Hall of Fame](/wiki/American_Theater_Hall_of_Fame "American Theater Hall of Fame") in 1979. For her roles in television she received two [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards") for Outstanding Single Performance by an Actress in a Leading Role in a Drama for her acting in the adaptations of [Truman Capote](/wiki/Truman_Capote "Truman Capote")'s *[A Christmas Memory](/wiki/A_Christmas_Memory "A Christmas Memory")* (1967) and *[The Thanksgiving Visitor](/wiki/The_Thanksgiving_Visitor "The Thanksgiving Visitor")* (1969).

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geraldine_Page&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Page was born November 22, 1924, in [Kirksville, Missouri](/wiki/Kirksville,_Missouri "Kirksville, Missouri"), the first child of Edna Pearl (née Maize) and Leon Elwin Page[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChristensenFoleyKremer1999590-1) who worked at Andrew Taylor Still College of Osteopathy and Surgery (combined with the American School of Osteopathy, eventually to form [A.T. Still University](/wiki/A.T._Still_University "A.T. Still University")). He was an author whose works included *Practical Anatomy* (1925), *Osteopathic Fundamentals* (1926), and *The Old Doctor* (1932).[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalter1992117-2) She had one younger brother, Donald.[[3]](#cite_note-nyt-3)

At age five, Page relocated with her family to [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChristensenFoleyKremer1999590-1) Raised a [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") by her mother, Page was an active parishioner of the Englewood Methodist Church in Chicago, where she had her first foray into acting within the church's theatre group, appearing in a play called Excuse My Dust, then playing Jo March in a 1941 production of [Louisa May Alcott](/wiki/Louisa_May_Alcott "Louisa May Alcott")'s *[Little Women](/wiki/Little_Women "Little Women")*.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarroll201359-4) After graduating from Chicago's [Englewood Technical Prep Academy](/wiki/Englewood_Technical_Prep_Academy "Englewood Technical Prep Academy"), she attended the [Goodman School of Drama](/wiki/The_Theatre_School_at_DePaul_University "The Theatre School at DePaul University") at the [Art Institute of Chicago](/wiki/Art_Institute_of_Chicago "Art Institute of Chicago") (which moved to [DePaul University](/wiki/DePaul_University "DePaul University") in 1978), with the intention of becoming an actress. Page had aspirations of becoming a pianist or visual artist, but at 17 she appeared in her first amateur theatre production, and from that point, she never wavered from her desire to be a professional actress.[[5]](#cite_note-britannica-5)

After graduating from the Art Institute of Chicago in 1945,[[6]](#cite_note-tulsa-6) Page studied acting at the Herbert Berghof School and the [American Theatre Wing](/wiki/American_Theatre_Wing "American Theatre Wing") in New York City,[[5]](#cite_note-britannica-5) studying with [Uta Hagen](/wiki/Uta_Hagen "Uta Hagen") for seven years,[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChristensenFoleyKremer1999590-1)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchechner196414-7) and then at the [Actors Studio](/wiki/Actors_Studio "Actors Studio") with [Lee Strasberg](/wiki/Lee_Strasberg "Lee Strasberg").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChristensenFoleyKremer1999590-1)[[8]](#cite_note-8) During this time, Page would return to Chicago in the summers to perform in [repertory theatre](/wiki/Repertory_theatre "Repertory theatre") in [Lake Zurich, Illinois](/wiki/Lake_Zurich,_Illinois "Lake Zurich, Illinois"), where she and several fellow actors had established their own independent theater company.[[5]](#cite_note-britannica-5) She also spent two critically successful years performing with a winter stock company called the Woodstock Players, another group from Goodman, who performed mostly at the [Woodstock Opera House](/wiki/Woodstock_Opera_House "Woodstock Opera House") where she was singled out by critic [Claudia Cassidy](/wiki/Claudia_Cassidy "Claudia Cassidy") of [The Chicago Tribune](/wiki/The_Chicago_Tribune "The Chicago Tribune") as destined to be a star to bear watching. During that time she was called "the lady with the thousand faces" for her ability to change her looks and actions to an extent that her most devoted fans were unable to recognize her.[[9]](#cite_note-9) While attempting to establish her career, she worked various odd jobs, including as a hat-check girl, theater usher, lingerie model, and a factory laborer.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChristensenFoleyKremer1999590-1)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geraldine_Page&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1945–1969: Early stage and film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geraldine_Page&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1945–1969: Early stage and film")]

Page, a trained [method actor](/wiki/Method_acting "Method acting"), spent five years appearing in various repertory theater productions in the Midwest and New York after graduating from college.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChristensenFoleyKremer1999590-1) On October 25, 1945, she made her New York stage debut in *Seven Mirrors*, a play devised by [Immaculate Heart High School](/wiki/Immaculate_Heart_High_School_(Los_Angeles) "Immaculate Heart High School (Los Angeles)") students from [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBanham1995833-10)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENathan1974142-11) The play ran for a total of 23 performances at Blackfriars Repertory Theatre on Manhattan's [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENathan1974142-11) In February 1952, director [José Quintero](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Quintero "José Quintero") cast Page in a minor role in *Yerma*, a theatrical interpretation of a poem by [Federico García Lorca](/wiki/Federico_Garc%C3%ADa_Lorca "Federico García Lorca"), staged at [Circle in the Square Theatre](/wiki/Circle_in_the_Square_Theatre "Circle in the Square Theatre") in New York City's [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village").[[12]](#cite_note-12) Page was subsequently cast in the role of Alma in the Quintero-directed production of *[Summer and Smoke](/wiki/Summer_and_Smoke "Summer and Smoke")*, written by [Tennessee Williams](/wiki/Tennessee_Williams "Tennessee Williams") (also staged at the Circle Theatre in 1952). Page's role in *Summer and Smoke* garnered her significant exposure, including a [Drama Desk Award](/wiki/Drama_Desk_Award "Drama Desk Award"),[[6]](#cite_note-tulsa-6) and a profile in *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Geraldine_Page_in_Hondo.png/250px-Geraldine_Page_in_Hondo.png)](/wiki/File:Geraldine_Page_in_Hondo.png)

Page in *[Hondo](/wiki/Hondo_(film) "Hondo (film)")* (1953)

Her official film debut and role in *[Hondo](/wiki/Hondo_(film) "Hondo (film)")*, opposite [John Wayne](/wiki/John_Wayne "John Wayne"), garnered her a nomination for the [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actress "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress"). Prior to this, she appeared in an uncredited role in *[Taxi](/wiki/Taxi_(1953_film) "Taxi (1953 film)")*. Speaking to a Kirksville newspaper, she said: "Actually *Hondo* wasn't my first movie. I had one small, but satisfactory scene in a Dan Dailey picture called *Taxi*, which was filmed in New York."[[14]](#cite_note-14) Page was [blacklisted in Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood_blacklist "Hollywood blacklist") after her debut in *Hondo* based on her association with [Uta Hagen](/wiki/Uta_Hagen "Uta Hagen") and did not work in film for nearly ten years.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChristensenFoleyKremer1999590-1) Her work continued on Broadway playing a spinster in the 1954–1955 production of *[The Rainmaker](/wiki/The_Rainmaker_(play) "The Rainmaker (play)")*, written by [N. Richard Nash](/wiki/N._Richard_Nash "N. Richard Nash"); and as the frustrated wife whose husband becomes romantically obsessed with a young Arab, played by [James Dean](/wiki/James_Dean "James Dean"), in the 1954 production of *[The Immoralist](/wiki/The_Immoralist_(play) "The Immoralist (play)")*, written by Augustus Goetz and [Ruth Goetz](/wiki/Ruth_Goetz "Ruth Goetz") and based on the [novel of the same name](/wiki/The_Immoralist "The Immoralist") (1902) by [André Gide](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Gide "André Gide").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrauss2014179-15) Page remained friends with Dean until his death the following year and kept a number of personal mementos from the play—including several drawings by him. After Page's death, these items were acquired by [Heritage Auctions](/wiki/Heritage_Auctions "Heritage Auctions") in 2006.[[16]](#cite_note-heritage-16) In 2015 [Angelica Page](/wiki/Angelica_Page "Angelica Page") revealed that her mother had an affair with Dean during the production of *[The Immoralist](/wiki/The_Immoralist_(play) "The Immoralist (play)")*.[[17]](#cite_note-17) She stated, "According to my mother, their affair went on for three-and-a-half months. In many ways my mother never really got over Jimmy. It was not unusual for me to go to her dressing room through the years, obviously many years after Dean was gone, and find pictures of him taped up on her mirror. My mother never forgot about Jimmy -- never. I believe they were artistic soul mates."[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/George_C._Scott_-_Geraldine_Page_-_1959.JPG/250px-George_C._Scott_-_Geraldine_Page_-_1959.JPG)](/wiki/File:George_C._Scott_-_Geraldine_Page_-_1959.JPG)

Page opposite [George C. Scott](/wiki/George_C._Scott "George C. Scott") in a 1959 *[NBC Sunday Showcase](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Showcase "NBC Sunday Showcase")* episode

Prior to [*Hondo*](/wiki/Hondo_(film) "Hondo (film)"), in 1952, she appeared in a revival of *[Summer and Smoke](/wiki/Summer_and_Smoke "Summer and Smoke")* in 1952 putting herself, the play, and director [Jose Quintero](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Quintero "José Quintero") at the beginning of the Off-Broadway scene. Page played the same role of Alma Winemiller in a 1953 radio version (opposite [Richard Kiley](/wiki/Richard_Kiley "Richard Kiley")) and a [film version in 1961](/wiki/Summer_and_Smoke_(film) "Summer and Smoke (film)") opposite [Laurence Harvey](/wiki/Laurence_Harvey "Laurence Harvey"). Both she and [Una Merkel](/wiki/Una_Merkel "Una Merkel") earned acting nominations for [Best Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actress "Academy Award for Best Actress") and [Best Supporting Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actress "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress") respectively in the [34th Academy Awards](/wiki/34th_Academy_Awards "34th Academy Awards") in 1961. The awards, however, went to [Sophia Loren](/wiki/Sophia_Loren "Sophia Loren") for *[Two Women](/wiki/Two_Women "Two Women")* and [Rita Moreno](/wiki/Rita_Moreno "Rita Moreno") for [*West Side Story*](/wiki/West_Side_Story_(1961_film) "West Side Story (1961 film)").

In 1959, Page earned an [Emmy](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award") nomination, of Best Single Performance by an Actress, for her role in the *[Playhouse 90](/wiki/Playhouse_90 "Playhouse 90")* episode "The Old Man", written by [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner").[[19]](#cite_note-19) She subsequently earned critical accolades for her performance in the 1959–1960 Broadway production of Tennessee Williams's *[Sweet Bird of Youth](/wiki/Sweet_Bird_of_Youth "Sweet Bird of Youth")* opposite [Paul Newman](/wiki/Paul_Newman "Paul Newman"), in which she originated the role of a larger-than-life, addicted, sexually voracious Hollywood legend trying to extinguish her fears about her career with a young hustler named Chance Wayne (played by Newman). For her performance, Page received her first nomination for the [Tony Award for Best Actress in a Play](/wiki/Tony_Award_for_Best_Actress_in_a_Play "Tony Award for Best Actress in a Play"), as well as the [Sarah Siddons Award](/wiki/Sarah_Siddons_Award "Sarah Siddons Award") for her performance in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_theatre "Chicago theatre").[[20]](#cite_note-20) She and Newman subsequently starred in the [1962 film adaptation of the same name](/wiki/Sweet_Bird_of_Youth_(1962_film) "Sweet Bird of Youth (1962 film)") and Page earned a nomination for the Academy Award for Best Actress for the film.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeintzelmanHoward2014365-21)

Geraldine Page won consecutive Golden Globe Awards for [Best Actress in a Motion Picture - Drama](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actress_in_a_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Actress in a Motion Picture – Drama") in [1961](/wiki/19th_Golden_Globe_Awards "19th Golden Globe Awards") and [1962](/wiki/20th_Golden_Globe_Awards "20th Golden Globe Awards") for [*Summer and Smoke*](/wiki/Summer_and_Smoke_(film) "Summer and Smoke (film)") and [*Sweet Bird of Youth*](/wiki/Sweet_Bird_of_Youth_(1962_film) "Sweet Bird of Youth (1962 film)"), respectively.

In 1963, Page starred in *[Toys in the Attic](/wiki/Toys_in_the_Attic_(1963_film) "Toys in the Attic (1963 film)")*, based on [Lillian Hellman](/wiki/Lillian_Hellman "Lillian Hellman")'s play of the same name, and garnered a [Golden Globe](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards") nomination. She received another nomination the following year starring in Delbert Mann's *[Dear Heart](/wiki/Dear_Heart "Dear Heart")* as a self-sufficient but lonely postmistress visiting New York City for a convention, finding love with a greeting card salesman. In 1964, she starred in a Lee Strasberg-directed Broadway revival of [Anton Chekhov](/wiki/Anton_Chekhov "Anton Chekhov")'s *[Three Sisters](/wiki/Three_Sisters_(play) "Three Sisters (play)")* playing eldest sister Olga to [Kim Stanley](/wiki/Kim_Stanley "Kim Stanley")'s Masha with [Barbara Baxley](/wiki/Barbara_Baxley "Barbara Baxley") as the interloper Natasha.[[22]](#cite_note-tcred-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) Both [Shirley Knight](/wiki/Shirley_Knight "Shirley Knight") and [Sandy Dennis](/wiki/Sandy_Dennis "Sandy Dennis") played the youngest sister Irina at different stages in this production.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPorter2006141-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Geraldine_Page_and_Truman_Capote_1966.png/250px-Geraldine_Page_and_Truman_Capote_1966.png)](/wiki/File:Geraldine_Page_and_Truman_Capote_1966.png)

Page with [Truman Capote](/wiki/Truman_Capote "Truman Capote"), 1966

Between 1966 and 1969, Page appeared in two holiday-themed television productions based on stories by [Truman Capote](/wiki/Truman_Capote "Truman Capote"): "The Christmas Memory" (for *[ABC Stage 67](/wiki/ABC_Stage_67 "ABC Stage 67")*) and the television film *[The Thanksgiving Visitor](/wiki/The_Thanksgiving_Visitor#Adaptation "The Thanksgiving Visitor")*, both of which earned her two consecutive Emmy Awards for Best Actress.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPugh201414-25)[[26]](#cite_note-baker-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) In 1967, Page appeared again onstage in [Peter Shaffer](/wiki/Peter_Shaffer "Peter Shaffer")'s *[Black Comedy/White Lies](/wiki/The_White_Liars_(Play) "The White Liars (Play)"),* a production which also included [Michael Crawford](/wiki/Michael_Crawford "Michael Crawford") and [Lynn Redgrave](/wiki/Lynn_Redgrave "Lynn Redgrave"), who were making their Broadway debuts.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBottoMitchell2002285–6-28) The same year, she appeared opposite [Fred MacMurray](/wiki/Fred_MacMurray "Fred MacMurray") in the [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney")-produced musical *[The Happiest Millionaire](/wiki/The_Happiest_Millionaire "The Happiest Millionaire")*.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChristensenFoleyKremer1999590-1) Bosley Crowther of *The New York Times* was critical of the film, noting: "Geraldine Page and [Gladys Cooper](/wiki/Gladys_Cooper "Gladys Cooper")...square off in one musical scene of socially up-staging each other that is drenched in perfumed vulgarity. But, then, the whole picture is vulgar. It is an over-decorated, over-fluffed, over-sentimentalized endeavor to pretend the lace-curtain millionaires are—or were—every bit as folksy as the old prize-fighters and the Irish brawlers in the saloon."[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Page starred opposite [Ruth Gordon](/wiki/Ruth_Gordon "Ruth Gordon") in the thriller *[What Ever Happened to Aunt Alice?](/wiki/What_Ever_Happened_to_Aunt_Alice%3F "What Ever Happened to Aunt Alice?")* (1969), the third and final film in the [Robert Aldrich](/wiki/Robert_Aldrich "Robert Aldrich")-produced trilogy which followed *[What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?](/wiki/What_Ever_Happened_to_Baby_Jane%3F_(1962_film) "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane? (1962 film)")* (1962) and *[Hush...Hush, Sweet Charlotte](/wiki/Hush...Hush,_Sweet_Charlotte "Hush...Hush, Sweet Charlotte")* (1964). The film is based on the novel *The Forbidden Garden* by [Ursula Curtiss](/wiki/Ursula_Curtiss "Ursula Curtiss") and features Page as Claire Marrable, a recently widowed socialite, who, discovers that her husband has left her virtually nothing. The widow hires a number of unsuspecting housekeepers whom she murders one by one and robs them of their life savings in order to keep up her extravagant lifestyle.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilver1995318-30) Writing for *The New York Times*, [Vincent Canby](/wiki/Vincent_Canby "Vincent Canby") deemed the film "an amusingly baroque horror story told by a master misogynist," and praised Page's "affecting" performance.[[31]](#cite_note-canby-31)

### 1970–1979: Mid-career work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geraldine_Page&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1970–1979: Mid-career work")]

Page subsequently appeared in the [Don Siegel](/wiki/Don_Siegel "Don Siegel")-directed thriller *[The Beguiled](/wiki/The_Beguiled_(1971_film) "The Beguiled (1971 film)")* (1971) opposite [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood"), playing the headmistress of a Southern girls' boarding school who takes in a wounded Union soldier.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESterritt201479–81-32) Director Siegel called Page "certainly as fine an actor as I've ever worked with. I never have gotten along better with anyone than I did with her."[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESterritt201479-33) This was followed by a supporting role in the comedy *[Pete 'n' Tillie](/wiki/Pete_%27n%27_Tillie "Pete 'n' Tillie")* (1972), for which she earned an Academy Award nomination for Best Supporting Actress.[[34]](#cite_note-34) She also appeared in three episodes of [Rod Serling](/wiki/Rod_Serling "Rod Serling")'s *[Night Gallery](/wiki/Night_Gallery "Night Gallery")* between 1972 and 1973.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuir2001627-35) In January 1973, she returned to Broadway playing [Mary Todd Lincoln](/wiki/Mary_Todd_Lincoln "Mary Todd Lincoln") opposite [Maya Angelou](/wiki/Maya_Angelou "Maya Angelou") in the two-character play *Look Away*, written by [Jerome Kilty](/wiki/Jerome_Kilty "Jerome Kilty").[[36]](#cite_note-36) In 1974, Page played Regina in a production of *"The Little Foxes"* in which she starred opposite her husband [Rip Torn](/wiki/Rip_Torn "Rip Torn") (in the role of Benjamin Hubbard) directed by Philip Minor. It was staged for the Academy Festival Theater at [Barat College](/wiki/Barat_College "Barat College") in [Lake Forest, Illinois](/wiki/Lake_Forest,_Illinois "Lake Forest, Illinois") and received a rave review from William Leonard of the *[Chicago Tribune](/wiki/Chicago_Tribune "Chicago Tribune")*: "Geraldine Page is giving one of the greatest performances of her glorious career in Lake Forest and she is surrounded by a cast so superb that the Academy Festival Theater's production of *"The Little Foxes"* becomes a powerful, searing, unforgettable show... it is a harrowing and ennobling evening in the theater-the kind that comes along all
too seldom. We have seen other stars in the role of the malevolently, ruthlessly scheming Regina Giddens—[Tallulah Bankhead](/wiki/Tallulah_Bankhead "Tallulah Bankhead") years ago in her greatest triumph, [Eileen Herlie](/wiki/Eileen_Herlie "Eileen Herlie") five seasons back at the Ivanhoe. Geraldine Page is a whole new story—I have seen Geraldine Page in innumerable roles, ever since she was playing in *[East Lynne](/wiki/East_Lynne "East Lynne")* with the Lake Zurich Players back in the '40s. I've never seen her more thrillingly convincing than in this production."[[37]](#cite_note-37)
The legendary [Kim Stanley](/wiki/Kim_Stanley "Kim Stanley") once said of Page's Regina that it "was possibly the finest performance" she had ever seen.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
Page received a nomination for the [Tony Award for Best Featured Actress in a Play](/wiki/Tony_Award_for_Best_Featured_Actress_in_a_Play "Tony Award for Best Featured Actress in a Play") (her second Tony Award nomination) for the 1975 production of [Alan Ayckbourn](/wiki/Alan_Ayckbourn "Alan Ayckbourn")'s *[Absurd Person Singular](/wiki/Absurd_Person_Singular "Absurd Person Singular")* with Sandy Dennis and [Richard Kiley](/wiki/Richard_Kiley "Richard Kiley").[[22]](#cite_note-tcred-22)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

She also had a supporting role as a charismatic Hollywood evangelist (modeled after [Aimee Semple McPherson](/wiki/Aimee_Semple_McPherson "Aimee Semple McPherson"))[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHischak201253-40) in *[The Day of the Locust](/wiki/The_Day_of_the_Locust_(film) "The Day of the Locust (film)")* (1975), an adaptation of the [Nathanael West](/wiki/Nathanael_West "Nathanael West") [novel of the same name](/wiki/The_Day_of_the_Locust "The Day of the Locust").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChristensenFoleyKremer1999590-1) In 1977, she appeared as a nun in the British comedy *[Nasty Habits](/wiki/Nasty_Habits_(film) "Nasty Habits (film)")*,[[41]](#cite_note-afi-41) and provided the voice role of the [villainess](/wiki/Villain "Villain") Madame Medusa in the [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Productions "Walt Disney Productions") animated film *[The Rescuers](/wiki/The_Rescuers "The Rescuers")*.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuinlan1987143-42) During this time, she also appeared on television, guest-starring in the popular series *[Kojak](/wiki/Kojak "Kojak")* (1976)[[43]](#cite_note-43) and *[Hawaii Five-O](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")* (1977).[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Page appeared as the mother of three siblings and wife of a prominent attorney in [Woody Allen](/wiki/Woody_Allen "Woody Allen")'s *[Interiors](/wiki/Interiors "Interiors")* (1978). For her performance, Page was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Actress,[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThise2008216-45) and won a [BAFTA Award for Best Actress in a Supporting Role](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Actress_in_a_Supporting_Role "BAFTA Award for Best Actress in a Supporting Role").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrystal2007628-46) *The New York Times*'s Vincent Canby lauded her performance in the film, writing: "Miss Page, looking a bit like a youthful [Louise Nevelson](/wiki/Louise_Nevelson "Louise Nevelson") with mink-lashed eyes, is marvelous — erratically kind, impossibly demanding, pathetic in her loneliness and desperate in her anger."[[47]](#cite_note-47) The following year, in November 1979, Page was inducted into the [American Theater Hall of Fame](/wiki/American_Theater_Hall_of_Fame "American Theater Hall of Fame").[[48]](#cite_note-hof-48)

### 1980–1986: Later work and final roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geraldine_Page&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1980–1986: Later work and final roles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/GERALDINE_PAGE_AND_BRIAN_CLARK_IN_THE_MADWOMAN_OF_CHAILLOT.jpg/250px-GERALDINE_PAGE_AND_BRIAN_CLARK_IN_THE_MADWOMAN_OF_CHAILLOT.jpg)](/wiki/File:GERALDINE_PAGE_AND_BRIAN_CLARK_IN_THE_MADWOMAN_OF_CHAILLOT.jpg)

Page with Brian Clark in a 1984 production of *[The Madwoman of Chaillot](/wiki/The_Madwoman_of_Chaillot "The Madwoman of Chaillot")*

Page starred as [Zelda Fitzgerald](/wiki/Zelda_Fitzgerald "Zelda Fitzgerald") in the last major Broadway production of a Williams play, *[Clothes for a Summer Hotel](/wiki/Clothes_for_a_Summer_Hotel "Clothes for a Summer Hotel")* in 1980,[[22]](#cite_note-tcred-22) followed by a supporting role in *[Harry's War](/wiki/Harry%27s_War_(1981_film) "Harry's War (1981 film)")* (1981). Page starred as the secretive nun Mother Miriam Ruth in the Broadway production of *[Agnes of God](/wiki/Agnes_of_God "Agnes of God"),* which opened in 1982 and ran for 599 performances with Page performing in nearly all of them; for her role, she received a nomination for the Tony Award for Best Actress in a Play.[[22]](#cite_note-tcred-22)

Also in 1983, [Sabra Jones Strasberg](/wiki/Sabra_Jones "Sabra Jones") and her husband [John Strasberg](/wiki/John_Strasberg "John Strasberg") founded [the Mirror Theater Ltd](/wiki/The_Mirror_Theater_Ltd "The Mirror Theater Ltd") and invited Page to accept the role of Founding Artist in Residence for its repertory program the Mirror Repertory Company.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Page remained continually active in theater, appearing in numerous repertory, Broadway, and Off-Broadway productions throughout the 1980s; this included roles in a revivals of *[Inheritors](/wiki/Inheritors_(play) "Inheritors (play)")* by [Susan Glaspell](/wiki/Susan_Glaspell "Susan Glaspell")[[50]](#cite_note-50) and *[Paradise Lost](/wiki/Paradise_Lost_(play) "Paradise Lost (play)")* by [Clifford Odets](/wiki/Clifford_Odets "Clifford Odets") in 1983,[[51]](#cite_note-51) *Rain* by [John Colton](/wiki/John_Colton_(screenwriter) "John Colton (screenwriter)") (based on the short story "[Miss Thompson](/wiki/Rain_(short_story) "Rain (short story)")" by [W. Somerset Maugham](/wiki/W._Somerset_Maugham "W. Somerset Maugham")) the following year.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Further revivals followed in 1985: *[Vivat! Vivat Regina!](/wiki/Vivat!_Vivat_Regina! "Vivat! Vivat Regina!")* by [Robert Bolt](/wiki/Robert_Bolt "Robert Bolt") (in which she played [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England")),[[53]](#cite_note-53) *Clarence* by [Booth Tarkington](/wiki/Booth_Tarkington "Booth Tarkington"),[[54]](#cite_note-54) and *[The Madwoman of Chaillot](/wiki/The_Madwoman_of_Chaillot "The Madwoman of Chaillot")* (by [Jean Giraudoux](/wiki/Jean_Giraudoux "Jean Giraudoux")) in which she played the Madwoman to great acclaim).[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Page earned her seventh Academy Award nomination for her performance in the dark comedy *[The Pope of Greenwich Village](/wiki/The_Pope_of_Greenwich_Village "The Pope of Greenwich Village")* (1984).[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThise2008218-56) This marked a record at the time for most Academy Award nominations without a win,[[57]](#cite_note-science-57) for which Page was tied with [Peter O'Toole](/wiki/Peter_O%27Toole "Peter O'Toole") and [Richard Burton](/wiki/Richard_Burton "Richard Burton") (who themselves had also garnered seven nominations without winning).[[58]](#cite_note-58) On television, Page had a supporting role in the miniseries *[The Dollmaker](/wiki/The_Dollmaker "The Dollmaker")* (1984), opposite [Jane Fonda](/wiki/Jane_Fonda "Jane Fonda") and [Amanda Plummer](/wiki/Amanda_Plummer "Amanda Plummer").[[59]](#cite_note-59) She appeared in the British [horror film](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film") *[The Bride](/wiki/The_Bride_(1985_film) "The Bride (1985 film)")* opposite [Sting](/wiki/Sting_(musician) "Sting (musician)") and [Jennifer Beals](/wiki/Jennifer_Beals "Jennifer Beals"); the drama *[White Nights](/wiki/White_Nights_(1985_film) "White Nights (1985 film)")*, directed by [Taylor Hackford](/wiki/Taylor_Hackford "Taylor Hackford"); and opposite [Rebecca De Mornay](/wiki/Rebecca_De_Mornay "Rebecca De Mornay") in the drama *[The Trip to Bountiful](/wiki/The_Trip_to_Bountiful "The Trip to Bountiful")* (all 1985), in which she played an aging Southern Texas woman seeking to return to her hometown. The role earned Page wide critical acclaim, with the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* referring to it as "the performance of a lifetime."[[26]](#cite_note-baker-26)

In 1986, she appeared on Broadway in *The Circle* by [W. Somerset Maugham](/wiki/W._Somerset_Maugham "W. Somerset Maugham"); during this production, Page won the [Academy Award for Best Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actress "Academy Award for Best Actress") for her performance in *The Trip to Bountiful*.[[3]](#cite_note-nyt-3) During her acceptance speech, she thanked [The Mirror Theater Ltd](/wiki/The_Mirror_Theater_Ltd "The Mirror Theater Ltd"). Page wore her costume from *The Circle*, which had been designed and made by Gail Cooper-Hecht, the Mirror Theater's costume designer.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosgrave2008172-60) She received the award from [F. Murray Abraham](/wiki/F._Murray_Abraham "F. Murray Abraham"), who, after winning his [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") for *[Amadeus](/wiki/Amadeus_(film) "Amadeus (film)")*, also joined the Mirror Repertory Company to play the rag-picker in *The Madwoman of Chaillot*.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Prior to winning the Academy Award, Page said to *[People](/wiki/People_(magazine) "People (magazine)")* magazine: "If I lose the Oscar this year, I'll have the record for the most nominations without ever winning... I'd love to be champion, [but the loser] doesn't have to get up there and make a fool of herself."[[62]](#cite_note-hutchings-62)

After winning the Academy Award, Page returned to finish her run performing in *The Circle* for [Mirror Theater](/wiki/The_Mirror_Theater_Ltd "The Mirror Theater Ltd") and appeared opposite [Carroll Baker](/wiki/Carroll_Baker "Carroll Baker"), [Oprah Winfrey](/wiki/Oprah_Winfrey "Oprah Winfrey"), and [Elizabeth McGovern](/wiki/Elizabeth_McGovern "Elizabeth McGovern") in *[Native Son](/wiki/Native_Son_(1986_film) "Native Son (1986 film)")* (1986).[[41]](#cite_note-afi-41) Page followed up *Native Son* with a lead role opposite [Mary Stuart Masterson](/wiki/Mary_Stuart_Masterson "Mary Stuart Masterson") in *[My Little Girl](/wiki/My_Little_Girl_(film) "My Little Girl (film)")* (1987).[[41]](#cite_note-afi-41) In the fall of 1986, Page asked permission to return to Broadway in a revival of [Noël Coward](/wiki/No%C3%ABl_Coward "Noël Coward")'s *[Blithe Spirit](/wiki/Blithe_Spirit_(play) "Blithe Spirit (play)")* in the role of Madame Arcati. She was cast in the role, though the production would be Page's last. She was again nominated for the Tony Award for Best Actress in a Play, though she did not win. A week after the Tony Awards ceremony, Page failed to appear for two performances of the play and was found dead in her Manhattan home.[[63]](#cite_note-fulton-63) The show lasted several weeks more, with Page's understudy [Patricia Conolly](/wiki/Patricia_Conolly "Patricia Conolly") taking over her role.[[3]](#cite_note-nyt-3)

## Reception and acting style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geraldine_Page&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reception and acting style")]

> If [other actors] have trained the way you've been trained there is at least the hope of communication. But wonderful actors are wonderful to act with–it doesn't matter how they've been trained.

Page on acting, 1964[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchechner1964125-64)

Page was trained as a [method actor](/wiki/Method_acting "Method acting"),[[6]](#cite_note-tulsa-6) and at times worked with [psychoanalysts](/wiki/Psychoanalysis "Psychoanalysis") when developing her interpretations of roles.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchechner1964125–6-65) She once told the *Los Angeles Times*: "If I read a part and think I can connect to it, that I can touch people with it, I will do it, no matter what its size. And if I think I can't do something with a part, I won't take it."[[63]](#cite_note-fulton-63) In a 1964 interview after completing the Broadway run of *The Three Sisters*, Page discussed her method acting at length.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchechner1964116–21-66) When asked if she used [emotional recall](/wiki/Affective_memory "Affective memory") as a technique, she responded: "I would never shut it out. But I don't try to get one. My whole effort is to relax and keep the doors open so that there's room if one should pop up."[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchechner1964126-67)

During her life, Page was regarded as a respected [character actress](/wiki/Character_actor "Character actor").[[57]](#cite_note-science-57) Speaking of her stage career in 1986, she said: "I used to think that by opening [night] all the work was done. Now I'm finding how much you can learn from the audience."[[57]](#cite_note-science-57) She described acting as a "bottomless cup", adding, "If I studied for the next ninety years I'd just be scratching the surface."[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchechner1964130-68)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geraldine_Page&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Page was married to violinist [Alexander Schneider](/wiki/Alexander_Schneider "Alexander Schneider") from 1954 to 1957.[[69]](#cite_note-tcm-69) On September 8, 1963, she married actor [Rip Torn](/wiki/Rip_Torn "Rip Torn"), who was six years her junior, in [Pinal, Arizona](/wiki/Pinal,_Arizona "Pinal, Arizona").[[70]](#cite_note-70) They had played opposite each other in *[Sweet Bird of Youth](/wiki/Sweet_Bird_of_Youth "Sweet Bird of Youth")* on Broadway and in the [1962 film](/wiki/Sweet_Bird_of_Youth_(1962_film) "Sweet Bird of Youth (1962 film)"). They had three children: a daughter, actress [Angelica Page](/wiki/Angelica_Page "Angelica Page"), and twin sons, actor [Anthony "Tony"](/wiki/Tony_Torn "Tony Torn") and Jonathan "Jon" Torn.

Beginning in the early 1980s, Page and Torn lived separately[[71]](#cite_note-torn-71) after he began an affair with actress [Amy Wright](/wiki/Amy_Wright "Amy Wright");[[72]](#cite_note-72) Torn had first met Wright in 1976 and began the affair shortly after.[[73]](#cite_note-spectacular-73) Page was aware of Torn and Wright's relationship, and had appeared onstage opposite Wright in the 1977 Off-Broadway production of *The Stronger*, under Torn's direction.[[73]](#cite_note-spectacular-73) In 1983, Torn fathered a child with Wright.[[73]](#cite_note-spectacular-73) After the birth of the child, Page was questioned about her marriage by columnist [Cindy Adams](/wiki/Cindy_Adams "Cindy Adams"), to which she responded: "Of course Rip and I are still married. We've been married for years. We're staying married. What's the big fuss?"[[69]](#cite_note-tcm-69) In spite of their separation, Page and Torn remained married until her death; her daughter described their relationship as still "close" up until Page died in 1987.[[71]](#cite_note-torn-71)

Page considered herself a [gourmand](/wiki/Foodie "Foodie"), once joking: "'Greedy Gut' is my middle name...Rip is wonderful. He does the cooking, and I do the eating. I love everything but eggplant."[[62]](#cite_note-hutchings-62)

## Death and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geraldine_Page&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Death and legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/425_West_22nd_Street.jpg/250px-425_West_22nd_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:425_West_22nd_Street.jpg)

Page's townhouse in [Chelsea, Manhattan](/wiki/Chelsea,_Manhattan "Chelsea, Manhattan"), where she died in 1987

On June 13, 1987, Page failed to arrive at the [Neil Simon Theatre](/wiki/Neil_Simon_Theatre "Neil Simon Theatre") for both the afternoon and evening performances of [Noël Coward](/wiki/No%C3%ABl_Coward "Noël Coward")'s *[Blithe Spirit](/wiki/Blithe_Spirit_(play) "Blithe Spirit (play)")*, which had begun its run in March.[[22]](#cite_note-tcred-22) At the end of the show's evening performance, the play's producer announced that Page had been found dead in her lower Manhattan townhouse.[[3]](#cite_note-nyt-3)[[63]](#cite_note-fulton-63) It was determined that she died of a [heart attack](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction").[[3]](#cite_note-nyt-3)

On June 18, "an overflow crowd of colleagues, friends and fans", including [Sissy Spacek](/wiki/Sissy_Spacek "Sissy Spacek"), [James Earl Jones](/wiki/James_Earl_Jones "James Earl Jones"), [Amanda Plummer](/wiki/Amanda_Plummer "Amanda Plummer"), [Jerry Stiller](/wiki/Jerry_Stiller "Jerry Stiller"), [Anne Meara](/wiki/Anne_Meara "Anne Meara") and husband Torn attended a memorial service held at the Neil Simon Theatre.[[74]](#cite_note-tribute-74)

Highlighting Page's achievements, actress [Anne Jackson](/wiki/Anne_Jackson "Anne Jackson") said, "[Page] used a stage like no one else I'd ever seen. It was like playing tennis with someone who had 26 arms."[[74]](#cite_note-tribute-74) Rip Torn called her "Mi corazón, mi alma, mi esposa" ("My heart, my soul, my wife") and said that they had "never stopped being lovers, and ... never will".[[74]](#cite_note-tribute-74)

## Acting credits and accolades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geraldine_Page&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Acting credits and accolades")]

Main articles: [Geraldine Page on screen and stage](/wiki/Geraldine_Page_on_screen_and_stage "Geraldine Page on screen and stage") and [List of awards and nominations received by Geraldine Page](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Geraldine_Page "List of awards and nominations received by Geraldine Page")

Page earned a total of eight nominations before winning her first [Academy Award for Best Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actress "Academy Award for Best Actress") in 1986 for *The Trip to Bountiful*.[[63]](#cite_note-fulton-63) She was also a winner of two [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"),[[75]](#cite_note-gg-75) two [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards"), and one [BAFTA](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Award "British Academy Film Award") award.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

For her stage work on Broadway, Page earned a total of four [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award") nominations,[[77]](#cite_note-tony-77) and was referred to by the *[New York Daily News](/wiki/New_York_Daily_News "New York Daily News")* as "one of the finest stage actors of her generation".[[71]](#cite_note-torn-71) She was inducted into the [American Theatre Hall of Fame](/wiki/American_Theatre_Hall_of_Fame "American Theatre Hall of Fame") in 1979.[[48]](#cite_note-hof-48)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geraldine_Page&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

[Sarah Paulson](/wiki/Sarah_Paulson "Sarah Paulson") portrayed Page in the 2017 anthology television series *[Feud](/wiki/Feud_(TV_series) "Feud (TV series)")*, which chronicles the rivalry between actresses [Bette Davis](/wiki/Bette_Davis "Bette Davis") and [Joan Crawford](/wiki/Joan_Crawford "Joan Crawford") on the set of *[What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?](/wiki/What_Ever_Happened_to_Baby_Jane%3F_(film) "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane? (film)")* (1962).[[78]](#cite_note-78)

She was also portrayed by her daughter, [Angelica Page](/wiki/Angelica_Page "Angelica Page"), in the stage production *[Turning Page](/wiki/Turning_Page "Turning Page")*. A [monologue](/wiki/Monologue "Monologue") play chronicling Page's life, it was also written by her daughter:[[79]](#cite_note-turning-79) "I grew up in the center of her sparkling career," Angelica recalled. "As her only daughter I feel compelled to share her lessons and gifts with others who did and did not have the opportunity to know her magic intimately. She was a true rebel and trail blazer. A masterful woman who was ahead of her time and should not be forgotten anytime soon."[[79]](#cite_note-turning-79) The play premiered in Los Angeles in 2016, followed by performances in New York City in 2017.[[79]](#cite_note-turning-79)
